did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed 'Melan-Dx', a knowledge-enhanced vision-language AI framework that augments frozen pathology vision-language models (PLIP, PathGen, CONCH, MUSK) using retrieval from a curated vision-knowledge database. It was used for differential diagnosis of melanocytic neoplasm pathology, performing binary classification (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma) and 40-class fine-grained classification on patch-level and whole-slide image (WSI) datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing 'Melan-Dx', a knowledge-enhanced vision-language framework. It details the curation of a new dataset (Penn Melan-Dx knowledge atlas with 2893 images), the design of a novel AI architecture using retrieval-augmented generation and contrastive learning, and reports quantitative results (accuracy, AUC, AUPRC, training time) from patch-level and whole-slide image classification experiments on melanocytic neoplasms.

title: Melan-Dx: a knowledge-enhanced vision-language framework improves differential diagnosis of melanocytic neoplasm pathology
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of differential diagnosis for melanocytic neoplasms by developing Melan-Dx, a knowledge-enhanced vision-language framework that augments frozen pathology foundation models (PLIP, PathGen, CONCH, MUSK) using retrieval from a curated vision-knowledge database. The authors curated the Penn Melan-Dx knowledge atlas, a structured dataset containing 2,893 high-quality pathology images and 1,102 expert-annotated knowledge entries across 44 disease classes. Melan-Dx improves diagnostic accuracy at both patch-level and whole-slide image (WSI) levels without fine-tuning the vision backbone, leveraging knowledge-enhanced contrastive learning. The framework achieved 0.869 accuracy for binary classification and 0.699 Top-1 accuracy for 40-class classification, with significant improvements over linear and fully finetuned baselines. Additionally, Melan-Dx demonstrated superior performance in WSI tasks, reaching 0.915 ROC AUC for few-shot and 0.925 AUPRC for fully supervised settings. These results indicate that integrating structured medical knowledge with vision-language models enhances diagnostic performance and training efficiency for melanocytic neoplasm pathology.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in binary classification (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma) and 40-class fine-grained classification. Specific subtypes listed in the Penn Melan-Dx knowledge atlas include Nodular (132 images), Lentigo maligna (255 images), Desmoplastic (179 images), Spitz melanoma (70 images), and others.

Benign melanocytic lesions used as comparators in binary classification and specific classes in 40-class classification. Examples include Dysplastic naevus (242 images), Junctional/compound/dermal naevi (172 images), Combined naevus (122 images), Spitz naevus (114 images), Blue naevus (89 images), and Pigmented spindle cell naevus (70 images).
Grouped under 'Spitz tumours' (311 images total) in the dataset. Subclasses include Spitz melanocytoma/Atypical Spitz tumour (57 images).

Specific benign/intermediate melanocytic tumors included in the 44 disease classes. WNT-activated deep penetrating/plexiform melanocytoma (89 images) and Pigmented epithelioid melanocytoma (87 images).
Used as the negative class in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma). Comprises all non-melanoma melanocytic neoplasms in the dataset.
Root-level category in the WHO classification hierarchy (115 images).
Root-level category in the WHO classification hierarchy (50 images).
Root-level category in the WHO classification hierarchy (48 images).
Root-level category in the WHO classification hierarchy (124 images).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma) and 40-class fine-grained histologic subtypes of melanocytic neoplasms.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma) achieving 0.869 accuracy with MUSK+Melan-Dx, and 40-class fine-grained classification achieving 0.699 Top-1 accuracy. It also evaluates whole-slide image (WSI) classification tasks using few-shot (0.915 ROC AUC) and fully supervised (0.925 AUPRC) settings.

datasets_used.name: Penn Melan-Dx knowledge atlas
datasets_used.description: A curated, structured dataset of high-quality pathology images and expert-annotated knowledge entries for melanocytic neoplasms, developed by the authors at the University of Pennsylvania. It integrates visual data, expert annotations, and hierarchical disease knowledge based on the WHO classification system. The dataset includes 2,893 pathology images (2,699 manually annotated by two board-certified dermatopathologists and 194 additional high-quality images from WHO classification materials) and 1,102 knowledge entries. It spans 44 distinct melanocytic neoplasm categories organized in a three-tier hierarchical structure.
classes_used: Melanocytic neoplasms in intermittently sun-exposed skin
Melanocytic neoplasms in chronically sun-exposed skin
Pigmented spindle cell naevus or Reed naevus
Low-CSD melanoma including superficial spreading melanoma

use.training: Used as the primary source for patch-level classification tasks (binary and 40-class) to train the retrieval-augmented Melan-Dx framework and evaluate performance against baselines.
use.validation: Used for validation within the experimental setup for patch-level classification tasks.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of patch-level classification performance (binary and 40-class) and ablation studies.
use.other: Used to construct the vision-knowledge database for retrieval-augmented generation. The knowledge entries were curated using GPT-4o and manually verified by experts.

datasets_used.name: HISTAI skin dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset providing case-specific diagnostic results for skin pathology. The authors filtered this dataset using GPT-4o to retain only cases with clear melanoma and nonmelanoma diagnoses, followed by manual verification. A subset of 1,000 whole-slide images (WSIs) was randomly selected for experiments.

use.training: Used for fully supervised WSI classification tasks.
use.validation: Used for validation in WSI classification tasks.
use.testing: Used for testing WSI classification performance in few-shot (6-shot settings) and fully supervised scenarios.
use.other: Used to evaluate the scalability and clinical applicability of the Melan-Dx framework at the whole-slide image level.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate uncertainty and generate 95% confidence intervals for patch-level classification performance metrics.

To assess statistical significance of performance differences between Melan-Dx and baseline methods (linear finetuned, fully finetuned, zero-shot) for patch-level classification, and between Melan-Dx and baselines for fully supervised WSI classification.
To confirm that removing any component of Melan-Dx resulted in statistically significant performance degradation in ablation studies.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in performance metrics between Melan-Dx and the baseline methods (linear finetuned, fully finetuned, zero-shot).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in performance metrics between Melan-Dx and the baseline methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was assessed using paired two-sided Student’s t-test with thresholds *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.005. Melan-Dx demonstrated statistically significant improvements over baseline approaches in nearly all cases. For comparisons where significance was not achieved, equivalent performance levels were confirmed. In ablation studies, removing any component resulted in statistically significant performance degradation.

binary_classification_melanoma_vs_nonmel.notes: P-values provided for comparisons against baselines. MUSK+Melan-Dx achieved the highest accuracy.
CONCH+Melan-Dx showed comparable performance to CONCH+Fully Finetuned (no statistically significant difference) but outperformed linear and zero-shot baselines.

40_class_fine_grained_classification.notes: Melan-Dx significantly outperformed all baseline methods across all foundation models.

40_class_fine_grained_classification_Top.notes: MUSK+Melan-Dx achieved the highest Top-3 accuracy.

40_class_fine_grained_classification_Hie.notes: Hierarchical accuracy reflects clinical consequences of diagnostic errors at higher taxonomic levels.

training_efficiency_binary_classificatio.notes: Melan-Dx reduced training time by 96.7% on average compared to fully finetuned approaches.

training_efficiency_40_class_classificat.notes: Melan-Dx reduced training time by 89.4% on average compared to fully finetuned approaches.

models.Melan-Dx (Ablated Configurations): Values lower than full model, P-values < 0.05

ablation_study_binary_classification_MUS.notes: Removing any component resulted in statistically significant performance degradation.

models.Melan-Dx (Ablated Configurations): Values lower than full model, P-values < 0.05

ablation_study_40_class_classification_M.notes: Removing any component resulted in statistically significant performance degradation.

few_shot_classification_CONCH.metric: ROC AUC / Accuracy / AUPRC / F1 Score
few_shot_classification_CONCH.notes: Peak performance for CONCH+Melan-Dx was at 128 shots for accuracy, AUPRC, and F1, and 64 shots for ROC AUC.

few_shot_classification_MUSK.metric: ROC AUC / Accuracy / AUPRC / F1 Score
few_shot_classification_MUSK.notes: Peak performance for MUSK+Melan-Dx was at 96 shots for all metrics. Few-shot performance exceeded MUSK supervised learning performance.

fully_supervised_classification_CONCH.metric: ROC AUC / Accuracy / F1 Score / AUPRC
fully_supervised_classification_CONCH.notes: Significant improvements in ROC AUC, Accuracy, and F1. AUPRC showed comparable performance (no significant difference).

fully_supervised_classification_MUSK.metric: ROC AUC / Accuracy / F1 Score / AUPRC

fully_supervised_classification_TITAN_ag.metric: ROC AUC / Accuracy / AUPRC / F1 Score
ROC AUC / Accuracy / AUPRC / F1 Score
fully_supervised_classification_TITAN_ag.notes: ROC AUC slightly lower than baseline (not significant). Significant improvements in Accuracy, AUPRC, and F1.

fully_supervised_classification_TITAN_ag.metric: ROC AUC / Accuracy / AUPRC / F1 Score
fully_supervised_classification_TITAN_ag.notes: Evaluated for completeness; Melan-Dx enhancement not applicable due to unavailability of text encoder.

architecture_used.pretraining: Utilizes frozen, out-of-the-shelf pathology foundation models: PLIP, PathGen, CONCH, and MUSK. The vision backbones are not fine-tuned.
architecture_used.input_size: 50x50 μm patches extracted from Whole Slide Images (WSI); patch-level images from the Penn Melan-Dx knowledge atlas.

architecture_details.framework_type: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and knowledge-enhanced contrastive learning
architecture_details.knowledge_base: Penn Melan-Dx knowledge atlas (2,893 images, 1,102 expert-annotated knowledge entries)
architecture_details.retrieval_mechanism: Retrieval from a curated vision-knowledge database using frozen vision-language models
components: Frozen vision encoder (PLIP, PathGen, CONCH, or MUSK)
Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) aggregation (for WSI, specifically ABMIL and TITAN)
architecture_details.fine_tuning: No fine-tuning of the vision backbone. Only the retrieval/knowledge enhancement components and linear heads are adapted/trained.

training_details.optimization_target: Linear classification layers and knowledge enhancement components (vision backbone frozen)
training_details.training_time_reduction: 96.7% for binary classification; 89.4% for 40-class classification compared to fully finetuned baselines

classification_or_output_head.wsi_level: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) aggregation (ABMIL or TITAN) followed by a linear classification layer

preprocessing_techniques: Patch extraction: 50x50 μm patches extracted from Whole Slide Images (WSI) for patch-level analysis.
Patch extraction: WSIs divided into 512x512 pixel patches at 10x magnification for WSI classification pipeline.
Data filtering: HISTAI skin dataset filtered using GPT-4o to retain only cases with clear melanoma and nonmelanoma diagnoses.
Manual verification: Diagnostic results in the filtered HISTAI dataset manually verified by experts.
Random selection: 1,000 WSIs randomly selected from the filtered HISTAI dataset for experiments.
conclusions: The Melan-Dx framework, which augments frozen pathology vision-language models (PLIP, PathGen, CONCH, MUSK) with retrieval from a curated vision-knowledge database, significantly improves differential diagnosis of melanocytic neoplasms without fine-tuning the vision backbone.

Melan-Dx achieved a binary classification accuracy of 0.869 (MUSK+Melan-Dx) and a 40-class fine-grained classification Top-1 accuracy of 0.699, outperforming linear finetuned, fully finetuned, and zero-shot baselines.
In whole-slide image (WSI) tasks, Melan-Dx demonstrated superior performance with a ROC AUC of 0.915 in few-shot settings and an AUPRC of 0.925 in fully supervised settings.
The framework provides substantial training efficiency gains, reducing training time by 96.7% for binary classification and 89.4% for 40-class classification compared to fully finetuned approaches.

Ablation studies confirmed that all components of the Melan-Dx architecture contribute significantly to performance, as removing any component resulted in statistically significant performance degradation.

The study highlights the clinical relevance of hierarchical accuracy metrics and demonstrates that integrating structured medical knowledge with vision-language models enhances diagnostic performance and computational efficiency for melanocytic neoplasm pathology.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a relatively small dataset size (2,893 images) due to the high cost and scarcity of expert dermatopathologist annotations required for fine-grained labeling.

The framework was evaluated exclusively on data from the University of Pennsylvania and the HISTAI skin dataset; external validation on independent, multi-institutional cohorts is lacking, raising concerns about generalizability to diverse populations or scanner types.

The HISTAI skin dataset used for WSI evaluation was filtered using GPT-4o and manually verified, introducing potential bias from the filtering model and limiting the scope to cases with 'clear' diagnoses, potentially excluding ambiguous real-world cases.
The paper is currently an 'Article in Press' (unedited version), which may contain errors that could affect content validity prior to final publication.

While the framework claims to avoid fine-tuning the vision backbone, it relies on a curated knowledge database generated with GPT-4o and expert verification, which introduces dependency on the quality and potential biases of the LLM and the specific expert annotators.

The performance gains in WSI tasks were evaluated using only two foundation models (CONCH and MUSK) and specific aggregation methods (ABMIL and TITAN), limiting the breadth of architectural comparisons.
